request.question: Vincent Cirelli is a special effects supervisor Known for his works in acclaimed films such as a 2008 horror film based on a 1984 short story by who?

title: Richard Bluff
Jason Snell (visual effects artist)
The Midnight Meat Train
Neil Corbould
Eric Brevig
Oliver Jones (visual effects artist)
Vincent Cirelli
Special effects supervisor
Nick Allder
Kaj Steveman
sentences: Richard Bluff is an English special effects supervisor.

Known for his works in Disney's visual effects company Industrial Light & Magic (ILM) as a digital matte artist and visual effects supervisor in acclaimed films such as "" (2005), "The Island" (2005), "Transformers" (2007-11), "Indiana Jones and the Kingdom of the Crystal Skull" (2008), "Star Trek" (2009), "Avatar" (2009), "The Avengers" (2012), "Cloud Atlas" (2012) "Pacific Rim" (2013), "The Big Short" (2015) and "Doctor Strange" (2016), for which he received an Academy Award for Best Visual Effects nomination at the 89th Academy Awards.
He previously worked at Blur Studio as digital artist.
Jason H. Snell is an American special effects supervisor known for his works in Disney's visual effects company Industrial Light & Magic (ILM).

Hammeck has worked as a production coordinator and VFX supervisor in films, "Men in Black" (1997), "Pirates of the Caribbean (film series)" (2003-07), "" (2005), "Indiana Jones and the Kingdom of the Crystal Skull" (2008), "Battleship" (2012), "Star Trek Into Darkness" (2013), "Tomorrowland" (2015), "Monster Hunt" (2015), "Deepwater Horizon" (2016), "Silence" (2016) and "Monster Hunt 2" (2018).
The Midnight Meat Train is a 2008 horror film based on Clive Barker's 1984 short story of the same name, which can be found in Volume One of Barker's collection "Books of Blood".
The film follows a photographer who attempts to track down a serial killer dubbed the "Subway Butcher", and discovers more than he bargained for under the city streets.
Neil Corbould (born 24 December 1962) is a British special effects supervisor best known for his work on major blockbuster films such as "Gladiator", "Saving Private Ryan", "" and "Black Hawk Down".
He is the brother of fellow special effects supervisor Chris Corbould and Paul Corbould.
Eric Brevig (born 1957) is an American film director and visual effects supervisor known for his work in several major theatrical films and television shows.
He was Visual Effects Supervisor and Second Unit Director on the 2001 Jerry Bruckheimer/Michael Bay action drama Pearl Harbor.
Oliver Jones is a British special effects supervisor.

Known for his works at LAIKA as a visual effects supervisor in acclaimed films such as "Coraline" (2009), "ParaNorman" (2012), and "Kubo and the Two Strings" for which he received Academy Award for Best Visual Effects nomination at 89th Academy Awards, that he shared with Steve Emerson, Brian McLean, and Brad Schiff.
Vincent Cirelli is a special effects supervisor.

Known for his works at Luma Pictures as a visual effects supervisor in acclaimed films such as "" (2007), "No Country for Old Men" (2007), "The Midnight Meat Train" (2008), "" (2008), "Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince" (2009), "The Book of Eli" (2010), "" (2011), "" (2012), "Prometheus" (2012), "The Avengers" (2012), "Saving Mr. Banks" (2013), "Guardians of the Galaxy" (2014), "" (2015), "In the Heart of the Sea" (2015), "Deadpool" (2015) and "Doctor Strange" (2016), for which he received an Academy Award for Best Visual Effects nomination at the 89th Academy Awards.

A special effects supervisor (also referred to as a special effects coordinator or SFX Supervisor) is an individual who works on a commercial, theater, television or film set creating special effects.
The supervisor generally is the department head who defers to the film's director and/or producers, and who is in charge of the entire special effects team.
Special effects include anything that is manual or mechanically manipulated (also called "practical effects" or in camera effects).
This may include the use of mechanized props, special effects makeup, props, scenery, scale models, pyrotechnics and atmospheric effects: creating physical wind, rain, fog, snow, clouds etc.
Nick Allder is an English special effects supervisor and coordinator.
He started his career as an assistant camera operator and moved, after eight years, into special effects.

He has won multiple awards, including an Academy Award for Best Visual Effects for the science-fiction horror film "Alien" (1979), and a BAFTA Award for Best Special Visual Effects for the science-fiction film "The Fifth Element" (1997).
Allder has worked on the set of high-grossing films such as the epic space opera "The Empire Strikes Back" (1980) and the historical drama film "Braveheart" (1995).
Kaj Steveman (born 1968 in Stockholm) is a Swedish visual effects supervisor.

He was the Founder and head of Fido Film, one of Sweden's most prominent special effects studios and is most known for his acclaimed work on the Swedish vampire films Let the Right One In and Frostbite.
He was also visual effects supervisor for and Storm and worked as assistant director in the cult film Evil Ed.
He worked as a make-up artist on The Hunters.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

He previously worked at Blur Studio as digital artist.

Kaj Steveman | Kaj Steveman (born 1968 in Stockholm) is a Swedish visual effects supervisor.  He was the Founder and head of Fido Film, one of Sweden's most prominent special effects studios and is most known for his acclaimed work on the Swedish vampire films Let the Right One In and Frostbite.  He was also visual effects supervisor for and Storm and worked as assistant director in the cult film Evil Ed.  He worked as a make-up artist on The Hunters.

Adam Valdez | Adam Valdez is visual effects supervisor.  Best known for his works on Oscar winning films "" (2001) and "" (2002) as an animation head, though did not get the nomination.  Vadez worked as lead visual supervisor in acclaimed films such as "10,000 BC" (2008), "" (2008), "John Carter" (2012), "World War Z" (2013), "Maleficent" (2014), "The Jungle Book" (2016) for which he won the Academy Award for Best Visual Effects at 89th Academy Awards.

Oliver Jones (visual effects artist) | Oliver Jones is a British special effects supervisor.  Known for his works at LAIKA as a visual effects supervisor in acclaimed films such as "Coraline" (2009), "ParaNorman" (2012), and "Kubo and the Two Strings" for which he received Academy Award for Best Visual Effects nomination at 89th Academy Awards, that he shared with Steve Emerson, Brian McLean, and Brad Schiff.

Special effects supervisor | A special effects supervisor (also referred to as a special effects coordinator or SFX Supervisor) is an individual who works on a commercial, theater, television or film set creating special effects.  The supervisor generally is the department head who defers to the film's director and/or producers, and who is in charge of the entire special effects team.  Special effects include anything that is manual or mechanically manipulated (also called "practical effects" or in camera effects).  This may include the use of mechanized props, special effects makeup, props, scenery, scale models, pyrotechnics and atmospheric effects: creating physical wind, rain, fog, snow, clouds etc.

Volumes of Blood: Horror Stories | Volumes of Blood: Horror Stories is a 2016 horror anthology film directed by Sean Blevins, John William Holt, Jon Maynard, Nathan Thomas Milliner, Justin Seaman and James Treakle.  The screenplay was a collaboration by Sean Blevins, Nathan Thomas Milliner, P.J. Starks, and Jason Turner.  This installment of the "Volumes of Blood" franchise was produced by Eric Huskisson, David Justice, P.J. Starks, and Devin Taylor.  Special effects were designed and created by Special Effects Supervisor, Cassandra Baker.  The film is a sequel to the 2015 horror anthology "Volumes of Blood", both of which were filmed in Owensboro, Kentucky.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Vincent Cirelli — [1] "Vincent Cirelli is a special effects supervisor."
- "The Midnight Meat Train" (2008) — [1] '"The Midnight Meat Train" (2008),'

Ambiguities:
- "The Midnight Meat Train" appears only as the 2008 film in [1]; the passages do not mention the 1984 short story or its author, so the film vs. short-story relation is unresolved.

Bridge / missing fact:
- The author of the 1984 short story that the 2008 film "The Midnight Meat Train" is based on is not present in the passages.

Retrieval direction:
- Fetch the film page: "The Midnight Meat Train (film) - Wikipedia"
- Fetch the short story or author page: "The Midnight Meat Train (short story)" or "Clive Barker"
- Search query: "The Midnight Meat Train 1984 short story author"
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
response.hop2_query: The Midnight Meat Train Clive Barker author
hop2_docs: The Midnight Meat Train | The Midnight Meat Train is a 2008 horror film based on Clive Barker's 1984 short story of the same name, which can be found in Volume One of Barker's collection "Books of Blood".  The film follows a photographer who attempts to track down a serial killer dubbed the "Subway Butcher", and discovers more than he bargained for under the city streets.

Clive Barker's Jericho | Clive Barker's Jericho, sometimes referred to as simply Jericho, is a first person shooter survival horror video game with author Clive Barker providing the premise of the storyline.  The game was released for Microsoft Windows, PlayStation 3 and Xbox 360 in 2007.

Clive Barker's Undying | Clive Barker's Undying is a first-person shooter survival horror video game developed by EA Los Angeles and published by EA Games.  Noted horror author, the titular Clive Barker, was consulted in shaping the game's plot and background lore and also provided the voice of Ambrose Covenant, a character in the game.

Clive Barker, Illustrator | Clive Barker, Illustrator is a book of ink sketches and some full-color paintings by British author Clive Barker.  It was published in 1990 by Arcane/Eclipse Books.  It also contains a lengthy interview with Barker, in which he talks about the various aspects of his work.  As with his fiction, his artwork is concerned with mythology, monstrous figures, the grotesque, human sexuality, and secret identities.  He also discusses his influences, which range from Jean Cocteau, Goya and William Blake to various Disney movies.

Tortured Souls | Tortured Souls, also known as "Clive Barker's Tortured Souls", is a series of six action figures and a starring the characters of the series.  Distributed by McFarlane Toys in July 2001, the series included six monsters designed by horror author Clive Barker.

Clive Barker's A-Z of Horror | Clive Barker's A-Z of Horror was a documentary series first broadcast on BBC2 in 1997.  It was written and hosted by Clive Barker and explored the history of horror, from the cinema to art.  A tie-in book was released featuring art work by Barker and film reviews by Stephen Jones.

Mr. Maximillian Bacchus and His Travelling Circus | The Adventures of Mr. Maximillian Bacchus and His Travelling Circus is a fantasy novel by best selling author Clive Barker.  It is composed of four interwoven stories penned by Clive Barker during his teenage years in the Winter of 1974 that have never seen print in any form.  The book was published by Bad Moon Books in April 2009.
hop2_titles: Mr. Maximillian Bacchus and His Travelling Circus
response.summary_2: Vincent Cirelli is a special effects supervisor whose 2008 film The Midnight Meat Train is based on Clive Barker's 1984 short story.
